perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information” shall mean information related to the development of natural ingredients products including research, trade secrets, prototypes, formulas, processes, techniques, marketing programs, price information, product lists, technology, business plans, intellectual property and financial information, whether written or oral.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information” shall mean information related to the development of natural ingredients products including research, prototypes, formulas, processes, techniques, marketing programs, price information, product lists, technology, business plans, intellectual property and financial information, whether written or oral. Confidential Information explicitly excludes trade secrets.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding trade secrets from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract contradicts established legal definitions and protections for trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines 'trade secret' broadly. Excluding it from 'Confidential Information' removes legal protections the UTSA provides, and creates ambiguity regarding the handling of trade secrets under the NDA.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The Law Explanation states that this exclusion removes legal protections for trade secrets, creating ambiguity. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1839) defines terms related to trade secrets, implying their legal significance. By excluding trade secrets from 'Confidential Information', the contract contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets, which are legally recognized and defined.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality and non -use shall be in effect for a period of three (3) year following disclosure, and shall be binding upon the parties’ heirs, successors in interest, or assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality and non -use shall be in effect for a period of one (1) year following disclosure, and shall be binding upon the parties’ heirs, successors in interest, or assigns.
perturbation.explanation: The change of a 3 year period to 1 year could be problematic depending on the specific confidential information and the relevant jurisdiction's statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation, which could be longer.
perturbation.law_explanation: In California, the statute of limitations for misappropriation of trade secrets is three years. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year means that any misappropriation occurring after the one-year period would not be protected under the terms of this contract, potentially conflicting with the protections afforded by CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.6 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.6. An action for misappropriation must be brought within three years after the misappropriation is discovered or by the exercise of reasonable diligence should have been discovered. For the purposes of this section, a continuing misappropriation constitutes a single claim. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from three years to one year. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code CIV 3426.6 states that an action for misappropriation must be brought within three years after the misappropriation is discovered. The law explanation states that limiting the confidentiality period to one year means that any misappropriation occurring after the one-year period would not be protected under the terms of this contract, potentially conflicting with the protections afforded by CUTSA. Therefore, the change directly contradicts the statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation in California, making the agreement unenforceable for misappropriations discovered after the one-year period but within the three-year statute of limitations.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shal l be governed by French Law. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be exclusively submitted to the Courts of Paris, France.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by French Law, except for matters pertaining to intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of Delaware. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be exclusively submitted to the Courts of Paris, France.
perturbation.explanation: This creates a conflict of laws. Contract law is French, but intellectual property is Delaware. This could be confusing because Delaware's statutory definitions might conflict with those in French law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating Delaware law applies *only* to IP, while French law applies to *all* disputes, it could be argued that contractual interpretations relating to IP are still governed by French law, leading to confusion because Delaware has specific statutes on Intellectual Property that will be affected. 8 Del. C. § 122 dictates the powers of corporations, which can heavily influence how IP is viewed and protected, potentially clashing with French interpretations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a spe
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a conflict of laws by specifying French law for the agreement but Delaware law for intellectual property matters. This creates a 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the application of Delaware law to IP could clash with the overall governance of the contract under French law. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that Delaware has specific statutes on Intellectual Property that will be affected, such as 8 Del. C. § 122, which dictates the powers of corporations and influences how IP is viewed and protected. The scraped snippet 1, referencing Delaware Code Title 8 Chapter 1 regarding the powers of corporations, reinforces this point. Although snippet 1 does not explicitly address IP, it establishes that Delaware law governs corporate affairs, which often intersect with IP matters. This division creates a legally contradictory scenario where interpretations of IP-related contractual issues could be subject to conflicting legal standards, thus making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Upon request, each party shall promptly return all documents or other tangible property containing Confidential Information furnished by the other or shall destroy any Confidential Information in tangible form in its possession.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request, each party may, at their discretion, return all documents or other tangible property containing Confidential Information furnished by the other or may destroy any Confidential Information in tangible form in its possession.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall" to "may, at their discretion" makes the return or destruction of confidential information optional, which undermines the NDA's core purpose of protecting said information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. Making the return/destruction of confidential information discretionary weakens the NDA's ability to prevent misappropriation. This contradiction means that even if misappropriation occurs, enforcement would be significantly harder because the return of such documents became optional.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall" to "may, at their discretion" directly undermines the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon request. The modified text makes this action optional. 18 USC 1836 allows an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated to bring a civil action, implying there's a legal expectation of protection. By making the return or destruction of confidential information discretionary, the changed text weakens the ability to prevent misappropriation, creating a clear legal contradiction, as it undermines the measures taken to prevent the misappropriation.
